title: Kinetic Energy
summary: The energy of motion. Learn KE = ½mv², feel why speed counts twice as much as mass, then use it as a tool to back out hidden forces, real stopping distances, and final speeds.

steps.title: The energy of motion
steps.body:
Anything that moves carries energy — the **energy of motion** — and we call it **kinetic energy** (KE).

You already feel it in everyday life. A tossed tennis ball barely nudges a box. A bowling ball gliding in at the *same speed* flattens it. Now speed that bowling ball up and it hits harder still.

So two things decide how much kinetic energy something has:

• how **heavy** it is (its mass), and
• how **fast** it's moving (its speed).

One of these two matters far more than the other — and that surprise is the whole point of this lesson.

steps.title: Putting a number on it
steps.body:
Here's how we measure kinetic energy. **m** is the mass in kilograms (kg), **v** is the speed in meters per second (m/s), and KE comes out in **joules (J)** — the same energy unit we used for work.

Look hard at that formula, because the most important thing about it is easy to miss: **v is squared, but m is not.** Speed doesn't just add to the energy — it *multiplies in twice*. That's why doubling the speed doesn't double the kinetic energy; it **quadruples** it.

Play with the explorer below. Drag the **mass** and **speed** sliders and watch KE respond, then try to hit the target. Notice how a small nudge to *speed* moves the bar far more than the same nudge to *mass*.

steps.prompt: A **2 kg** block slides across the floor. Drag its **speed** and watch the kinetic energy respond — this is the v² effect in action.
steps.task: Drag the speed to **double** it — from 3 m/s to 6 m/s. Then compare the two bars.
steps.solution: Kinetic energy is KE = ½mv², and the speed is squared. Doubling the speed from 3 m/s to 6 m/s makes the v² term grow by 2² = 4, while the mass stays fixed at 2 kg. So KE jumps from 9 J to **36 J — four times as much**. That factor-of-four is the single most important idea in this lesson.

steps.prompt: Warm-up. A **3.0 kg** bowling ball rolls down the lane at **4.0 m/s**. How much kinetic energy does it carry?
hints: Drop the numbers straight into KE = ½mv² — but square the speed *before* you multiply.
steps.solution:
Use the kinetic energy formula directly, remembering to square the speed first:

KE = ½mv² = ½ × 3.0 × 4.0² = ½ × 3.0 × 16 = **24 J**.

steps.title: KE is a problem-solving tool
steps.body:
Kinetic energy isn't just a number to compute — it's a *lever*. Pair it with the **work-energy theorem** from Lesson 1 and you can find **forces** and **distances** without ever touching a kinematics equation.

Remember the theorem: the net work done on an object equals its change in kinetic energy. Written out, that's the difference between the final and starting KE:

For something that starts **from rest** (v_0 = 0), the second term vanishes and it simplifies beautifully: F·d = ½mv². Whatever you're missing — the force, the distance, or the final speed — you just rearrange to solve for it.

steps.prompt: You push a loaded sled (total mass **20 kg**) from rest across smooth, level snow. After you've pushed it **8.0 m**, it's gliding at **6.0 m/s**. Ignoring friction, what average force did you apply?
hints: The sled starts at rest, so all the work you do turns into its final kinetic energy: F_A·d = ½mv².
Find the KE first: ½ × 20 × 6.0² = 360 J. Then F_A = work ÷ distance = 360 ÷ 8.0.
steps.solution:
On smooth snow your push is the only horizontal force, so the work you do equals the sled's gain in kinetic energy: F_A·d = ΔKE = ½mv².

First the KE: ½ × 20 × 6.0² = ½ × 20 × 36 = 360 J. The sled started from rest, so you did 360 J of work over 8.0 m.

F_A = ΔKE / d = 360 / 8.0 = **45 N**.

steps.prompt: You push a small **0.50 kg** sled from rest along smooth, level ice, pressing with a steady **5.0 N** over a distance of **5.0 m**. How fast is the sled moving when you let go?
hints: From rest, all the work you do becomes kinetic energy: F_A·d = ½mv². This time solve for v.
steps.solution:
Starting from rest, every joule of work you do becomes kinetic energy: F_A·d = ½mv².

First find the work done: F_A·d = 5.0 × 5.0 = 25 J. Then solve for the speed: v = √(2·F_A·d / m) = √(2 × 25 / 0.50) = √100 = **10 m/s**.

steps.title: Braking: where the energy goes
steps.body:
If positive work *adds* kinetic energy, what removes it? **Negative work** — a force pointing against the motion.

When a car brakes, friction between the tires and the road points backward, opposite to the way the car is moving. It does negative work, draining the car's kinetic energy and turning it into heat in the brakes and tires. The car stops only once **every joule of KE has been removed**.

That gives us a clean way to find a stopping distance. The friction force *F_f* acting over the stopping distance *d* must remove all the starting kinetic energy:

Notice that KE here is always positive — kinetic energy itself is *never* negative. It's the *work* done by friction that's negative, because friction and motion point opposite ways.

steps.prompt:
**Setup:** A **1200 kg** car travels at **24 m/s**. When the driver brakes hard, the road applies a constant **7200 N** friction force.

**Part A —** How far does the car travel before it stops?
hints: To stop the car, friction must remove all of its kinetic energy: F_f·d = ½mv². Solve for d.
steps.solution:
Friction does negative work that removes the car's entire kinetic energy, so F_f·d = ½mv².

KE = ½ × 1200 × 24² = ½ × 1200 × 576 = 345,600 J. Then d = KE / F_f = 345,600 / 7200 = **48 m**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** Same car, same brakes — but now it's driving **twice as fast, at 48 m/s**. How far does it travel before stopping now?
hints: Stopping distance is set by the kinetic energy friction must remove — and KE depends on v². So what does doubling the speed do?
Double the speed → 2² = 4× the KE → 4× the stopping distance. That's 4 × 48 m.
steps.solution:
The stopping distance is set by the kinetic energy that friction has to remove, and KE = ½mv² grows with the *square* of speed.

Doubling the speed quadruples the KE (2² = 4), so the stopping distance also quadruples: 4 × 48 = **192 m**.

This is exactly why a small increase in speed is so dangerous on the road — the energy to dissipate, and the distance to do it, climb far faster than the speedometer suggests.

steps.title: Speed beats mass — every time
steps.body:
Because v is squared and m is not, **speed is the heavyweight** in the kinetic-energy formula. A lighter object moving fast can easily out-punch a heavier object crawling along.

Follow the energy through the chain below. Watch how the same amount of kinetic energy can be reached by a big slow mass *or* a small fast one — but the fast one gets there with a lot less mass to spare.

Keep this in your back pocket for the next problem: don't assume the heavier object always carries more KE. Run the numbers — and remember to square the speed.

steps.prompt: Two objects are moving. **Object A** is a heavy **4.0 kg** ball rolling at **3.0 m/s**. **Object B** is a light **1.0 kg** ball whizzing along at **6.0 m/s**. How much kinetic energy does the *lighter, faster* Object B have?
hints: Just apply KE = ½mv² to Object B — but square its speed before multiplying.
steps.solution:
Apply the formula to Object B, squaring the speed first:

KE_B = ½ × 1.0 × 6.0² = ½ × 1.0 × 36 = **18 J**.

Here's the punchline: Object A has KE = ½ × 4.0 × 3.0² = 18 J — *exactly the same*. Object B is one-quarter the mass, but moving twice as fast, and because v is squared those two effects cancel out. Speed really does carry its weight.

steps.prompt:
**Challenge — put it all together.** A **8.0 kg** crate starts from rest. You push it **6.0 m** across a level floor with a steady horizontal **40 N**, while friction drags back with a constant **16 N**.

**Part A —** What is the *net* work done on the crate over those 6.0 m?
hints: Your push does positive work; friction does negative work. The net force along the floor is F_A − F_f.
Net force = 40 − 16 = 24 N. Net work = that net force × the 6.0 m.
steps.solution:
Your push helps the motion (positive work) while friction opposes it (negative work), so the net force along the floor is F_A − F_f = 40 − 16 = 24 N.

W_net = (F_A − F_f)·d = 24 × 6.0 = **144 J**.

steps.prompt: **Part B —** The crate started from rest and the net work on it was **144 J**. Using the work-energy theorem, how fast is the **8.0 kg** crate moving after the 6.0 m?
hints: From rest, all the net work becomes kinetic energy: W_net = ½mv². Solve for v.
steps.solution:
Because the crate starts from rest, the work-energy theorem gives W_net = ΔKE = ½mv².

Solve for the speed: v = √(2·W_net / m) = √(2 × 144 / 8.0) = √36 = **6.0 m/s**.

That's the full toolkit in one problem: combine forces into a net work, then convert that net work straight into a final speed.

steps.title: Lesson complete!
steps.body:
You built kinetic energy from scratch: what it is, why **v² makes speed count twice as much as mass**, and how to wield KE = ½mv² as a tool. You backed out a hidden force, solved for a launch speed, found a real stopping distance, saw why doubling speed quadruples it, and finished by chaining net work into a final speed.

Next up: where does energy hide when nothing's moving? Time for **gravitational potential energy**.
